---
title: "Version Ranges"
tagline: "Or what to Do When You Want the Latest and Greatest (Until It Breaks)"
type: scenario
tool: sbt
---

#### ! Scenario

*Or what to Do When You Want the Latest and Greatest (Until It Breaks)*
This scenario demonstrates how to use version ranges in SBT.

#### ! Example

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-a:1.+]
```

#### ! Example

```{.scala include="build.sbt" snippet="s9-deps"}
```

#### ! Expect
Automatically resolve the latest patch or version in the 1.x series (e.g., `1.+`).

#### ! Why
SBT (via Ivy/Coursier) queries repository metadata and selects the highest version satisfying the range. This makes the build dynamic and dependent on current repository state.

#### ! Risk
* LOW Use ranges for `Test` dependencies.
* MEDIUM Use ranges for internal libraries under strict semver.
* HIGH Use ranges for external libraries; non-deterministic builds risk runtime "AbstractMethodError" failures during automated redeploys.

#### ! Check
**Fast check:** `sbt dependencyTree`

**What to look for:** The resolved version in the dependency tree.

**Conclusion:** Ranges trade build reproducibility for freshness.

#### ! Takeaway
Ranges provide automatic upgrades but sacrifice build reproducibility. Use dependency locking to mitigate the risk.

#### Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 11: Locking](../scenario-11-locking/guide.qmd) - How to freeze dynamic versions.

#### ! Wild

A well-known failure mode with version ranges occurs when CI resolves a newly published dependency that developer machines have not yet seen. SBT uses [Coursier](https://get-coursier.io/) for dependency resolution, which maintains its own local cache separate from Maven's. The cache TTL and refresh behaviour differ between developer machines and CI environments, so a version range like `[1.0,2.0)` can resolve to different concrete versions depending on when and where the build runs. The build fails without any source changes. SBT's [`dependencyLock`](https://www.scala-sbt.org/1.x/docs/Library-Management.html) or third-party plugins like sbt-dependency-lock can freeze resolution results to prevent this drift.
